class-roster-cleaner.label: Course Capstone: Class Roster Cleaner
class-roster-cleaner.summary: The school office has a messy roster export before report day. Build the Python tool that cleans it, skips bad rows, summarizes scores, and writes the final reports.

sketch0.title: Your capstone client: the school office

cp_4.title: Move cleaning into a helper module

cp-1-read-roster-file.title: Read the roster file
cp-1-read-roster-file.prompt: The school office sends you `data/roster.csv` and says, “Before we trust this file, can you show us exactly what it contains?” Read the file and print every raw line without extra blank lines, including the header row.
cp-1-read-roster-file.hint: Start by inspecting the raw export exactly as it arrived.

help.concept: A careful data-cleaning project begins with visibility: read the source file before transforming it.
help.hint_2: Loop through the file and print `line.strip()`.

cp-1-read-roster-file.starterCode: # Read data/roster.csv and print every raw line without extra blank lines.

cp-2-clean-one-row.title: Clean one roster row
cp-2-clean-one-row.prompt: The office wants each roster row cleaned into dependable fields. Write `clean_row(line)` for one roster row. Return a dictionary with cleaned `name`, integer `score`, and lowercase `email`, then print the cleaned first data row.
cp-2-clean-one-row.hint: Turn one messy row into one predictable dictionary.

help.concept: Once one row has a dependable shape, the whole project can reuse that same cleaning logic for every row.
help.hint_2: Use `.strip().title()` for names, `int(...)` for scores, and `.strip().lower()` for emails.

cp-2-clean-one-row.starterCode:
def clean_row(line):
    # TODO: split name, score, and email. Return a dictionary.
    pass

with open("data/roster.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    first_row = next(file)

print(clean_row(first_row))

cp-3-skip-bad-roster-rows.title: Skip bad rows safely
cp-3-skip-bad-roster-rows.prompt: Some rows in the office export have bad scores. Build a list of cleaned roster records and use `try`/`except` so invalid rows are skipped instead of crashing the program.
cp-3-skip-bad-roster-rows.hint: The office needs the good rows even if a few rows are broken.

help.concept: Real files are rarely perfect. A useful data tool protects the workflow by collecting valid records and skipping rows it cannot clean.
help.hint_1: Create `records = []`, then loop through the data rows after the header.
help.hint_2: Append cleaned rows inside `try`, and skip invalid rows in `except ValueError`.

cp-4-move-cleaning-to-module.title: Move cleaning into a helper module
cp-4-move-cleaning-to-module.prompt: The roster cleaner is growing, so move the cleaning logic into `tools/cleaning.py`. Import it in `main.py` and keep the cleaned records output exactly the same.
cp-4-move-cleaning-to-module.hint: Move the helper without changing what the program prints.

help.concept: When a project grows, helper modules keep the main file focused on the workflow instead of the details.

cp-5-add-summary-functions.title: Add summary functions
cp-5-add-summary-functions.prompt: The office now asks, “How is this class doing overall?” Add scoring helpers in `tools/scoring.py`, then print the average score rounded to one decimal place and the passing count.
cp-5-add-summary-functions.hint: Keep summary math in helper functions so `main.py` stays readable.

help.concept: Reports usually need summaries, not just cleaned rows. Helper functions make those calculations easy to test and reuse.
help.hint_2: Import both helpers in `main.py` and print the required summary lines.

cp-5-add-summary-functions.starterCode:
from tools.cleaning import clean_row
from tools.scoring import average_score, passing_count

records = []
with open("data/roster.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    for line in file:
        try:
            records.append(clean_row(line))
        except ValueError:
            continue

# TODO: print the average score and passing count.

cp-6-write-final-reports.title: Write final reports
cp-6-write-final-reports.prompt: Finish the client deliverable. Use helper modules to clean the roster, write `output/clean_roster.csv`, write `output/summary.txt`, and print the final summary for the school office.
cp-6-write-final-reports.hint: This is the handoff step: clean the data, write the files, and print a clear completion summary.

help.concept: A real project ends with deliverables: cleaned files and a summary someone else can use.
help.hint_2: Make sure both output files are created and the final printed summary includes the cleaned record count.

cp-6-write-final-reports.starterCode:
from tools.cleaning import clean_row
from tools.scoring import average_score, passing_count
from tools.reports import write_clean_roster, write_summary

records = []
with open("data/roster.csv") as file:
    next(file)
    for line in file:
        try:
            records.append(clean_row(line))
        except ValueError:
            continue

# TODO: write output/clean_roster.csv and output/summary.txt.
print(f"Cleaned {len(records)} records")

try-class-roster-cleaner-sketch0.title: Preview a roster row
try-class-roster-cleaner-sketch0.prompt: Clean one roster row before building the full capstone app. Finish the small program, run it, and check that the result fits the scenario.
try-class-roster-cleaner-sketch0.hint: Finish the small program in `main.py`, then run it to check that it behaves like the task asks.

help.concept: This Try it yourself checkpoint turns the lesson idea from "Build a real data-cleaning project" with a small runnable program.
help.hint_1: Use the TODO comments as your checklist for this small job.
help.hint_2: Make sure the final program shows the result the scenario needs.

sketch-1.title: Your capstone client: the school office
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
Report day is coming, and the school office has a roster export that is not ready to use. Names have inconsistent spacing and capitalization, scores include bad values, and the staff needs clean files they can trust.

In this capstone, you will build the tool they asked for. The project grows smoothly from one raw file reader into a multi-file cleaner with helper modules, summary functions, and final report files. Each step starts from the previous canonical solution so the work feels like one real project, not a pile of disconnected exercises.
